text:
Which of the following reactions produce ethyl cyanide as a major product?

1. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH=NOH} \xrightarrow{(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O}}\)
2. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Ethanolic KCN}}\)
3. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Ethanolic AgCN}}\)
4. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CON(CH}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{SO}_2\text{Cl/Pyridine, 70°C}}\)
explanation:
- **Reaction 1:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH=NOH} \xrightarrow{(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CN}\) (Beckmann-type dehydration with acetic anhydride converts aldoxime to nitrile = **ethyl cyanide**) ✓

- **Reaction 2:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{KCN} \xrightarrow{\text{ethanolic}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CN}\) (KCN is a carbon nucleophile → **alkyl cyanide** as major product) ✓

- **Reaction 3:** Ethanolic **AgCN** — Ag⁺ makes CN⁻ a nitrogen nucleophile → gives **isocyanide** (CH₃CH₂NC) as major product ✗

- **Reaction 4:** No reaction occurs under these conditions ✗

Reactions **1 and 2** give ethyl cyanide as major product.
name: Questions Item 1

text:
The order of boiling points of the following amine compounds is:

1. \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{N}\)  2. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2\)  3. \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NHCH}_3\)
explanation:
Boiling points of amines depend on van der Waals' forces and hydrogen bonding. Van der Waals' forces increase with surface area and decrease with branching.

| Compound | Type | H-bonding | Branching |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2: \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2\) | 1° amine | 2 N—H bonds | Least branched | **highest BP** |
| 3: \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NHCH}_3\) | 2° amine | 1 N—H bond | Moderate |
| 1: \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{N}\) | 3° amine | No N—H | Most branched | **lowest BP** |

Boiling point order: **2 > 3 > 1**
name: Questions Item 2

text:
What will be the product A in the reaction?

\(\text{Aniline} + \text{HNO}_2 \rightarrow A + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\)
explanation:
This is a **diazotisation reaction**. Aniline (aromatic primary amine) reacts with nitrous acid (\(\text{HNO}_2\), generated in situ from NaNO₂/HCl) at low temperature (0–5°C) to form a **diazonium salt**:

\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + \text{HNO}_2 \xrightarrow{0-5°\text{C}} \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^-}_{\text{Benzene diazonium chloride}} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

Benzene diazonium salt is highly reactive and forms the basis of many organic synthesis reactions (Sandmeyer, Gattermann, coupling, etc.).
name: Questions Item 3

text: Presence of which of the following is suitable for the synthesis of bromobenzene from benzene diazonium salts?
explanation:
The synthesis of bromobenzene from benzene diazonium chloride using **\(\text{Cu}_2\text{Br}_2\)** (cuprous bromide) and HBr is known as the **Sandmeyer reaction**:

\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}_2\text{Br}_2, \text{HBr}, -\text{N}_2} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{Br}\]

Cuprous bromide acts as the copper catalyst that transfers the bromide ion to the phenyl group. This is a standard method for introducing halogens into aromatic rings at specific positions without the electrophilic substitution limitations.
name: Questions Item 4

text: Which of the following statements about aniline is FALSE?
options: Aniline is a stronger base than ammonia.
Aniline is less basic than methylamine.
\(pK_b\) of aniline is more than that of ammonia.
Aniline reacts with bromine water to form a white precipitate.
explanation:
**Statement A — FALSE (the answer):** Aniline is actually a **weaker base** than ammonia, NOT stronger. The lone pair of electrons on N in aniline is **delocalised into the benzene ring** through resonance, making it less available for donation to a proton. Since ammonia has its full lone pair available, **NH₃ is a stronger base** than aniline.

**Statement B — Correct:** Aniline (pKₐ ~4.6) is less basic than methylamine (pKₐ ~10.6) ✓

**Statement C — Correct:** Higher pKb means weaker base. Aniline has pKb ~9.4 > NH₃ pKb ~4.7 ✓

**Statement D — Correct:** Aniline + Br₂(aq) → 2,4,6-tribromoaniline (white precipitate) ✓
name: Questions Item 5

text:
Which of the following statements about sulphonamides is TRUE?

(i) Tertiary amines react with acid chlorides.
(ii) N-ethyl benzene sulphonamide is soluble in alkali.
(iii) N,N-diethyl benzene sulphonamide is soluble in alkali.
(iv) Tertiary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent.
explanation:
- **(i) Incorrect:** Tertiary amines (R₃N) have no N—H bond, so they cannot react with acid chlorides via the usual acylation mechanism.

- **(ii) Correct:** **N-ethyl benzene sulphonamide** has an N—H bond. The hydrogen on nitrogen in this sulphonamide is **strongly acidic** (due to the electron-withdrawing effect of —SO₂—) and reacts with NaOH to form a soluble sodium salt. ✓

- **(iii) Incorrect:** N,N-diethyl benzene sulphonamide has **no N—H bond** → not acidic → not soluble in alkali.

- **(iv) Incorrect:** Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent (sulfonyl chloride) because they lack N—H bonds.

Correct: **(ii)**
name: Questions Item 6

text: In the preparation of a 1°-amine from an alkyl halide, with simultaneous addition of one —CH₂ group to the carbon chain, the reagent used as source of nitrogen is
explanation:
**Potassium cyanide (KCN)** is the reagent that simultaneously:
1. Introduces a nitrogen atom (as —CN)
2. **Adds one —CH₂ group** to the carbon chain (CN becomes CH₂NH₂ after reduction)

Reaction scheme:
\[\text{R—X} + \text{KCN} \rightarrow \text{R—CN} + \text{KX}\]
\[\text{R—CN} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni or LiAlH}_4} \text{R—CH}_2\text{—NH}_2\]

The nitrile (—CN) contains both the extra carbon AND the nitrogen. After reduction, we get a primary amine with **one more carbon** than the starting alkyl halide.
name: Questions Item 7

text: Acetic acid on heating with NH₃ forms A. When A reacts with LiAlH₄ followed by hydrolysis gives B. When B is heated with chloroform in KOH medium gives C. What are B and C respectively?
explanation:
**Step 1:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-\text{NH}_4^+ \xrightarrow{\Delta} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CONH}_2}_{A\text{ (acetamide)}}\)

**Step 2:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CONH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{LiAlH}_4, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2}_{B\text{ (ethylamine)}}\) (amide reduced to primary amine)

**Step 3 — Carbylamine reaction:** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{CHCl}_3 + 3\text{KOH} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NC}}_{C\text{ (ethyl isocyanide)}} + 3\text{KCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}\)

B = **ethylamine (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2\))**, C = **ethyl isocyanide (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NC}\))**
name: Questions Item 8

text:
Identify X and Y in the following reactions:

\(X \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 \xleftarrow{\text{Br}_2/\text{NaOH}} Y\)
explanation:
The target is **propylamine** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2\), 3 carbons).

**X via H₂/Ni reduction:**
\[X \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2\]
H₂/Ni reduces —CN to —CH₂NH₂ (adds a CH₂). So X has **2 carbons**: \(\text{X} = \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CN}\) (propionitrile)

**Y via Hofmann bromamide reaction (Br₂/NaOH):**
\[Y \xrightarrow{\text{Br}_2/\text{NaOH}} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2\]
Hofmann reaction: amide → primary amine (loses one carbon). Y has **4 carbons**: \(\text{Y} = \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CONH}_2\) (butyramide)

X = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CN}\)**, Y = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CONH}_2\)**
name: Questions Item 9

text: If the general formula of oxime and semicarbazone is C=N—Z, what is 'Z' in oxime (I) and semicarbazone (II)?
explanation:
The general formula for these condensation products with carbonyl compounds is **C=N—Z**:

**Oxime (I):** Formed from carbonyl + **hydroxylamine (NH₂OH)**:
\[\text{R—CHO} + \text{NH}_2\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{R—CH=N—OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}\]
So Z in oxime = **—OH** ✓

**Semicarbazone (II):** Formed from carbonyl + **semicarbazide (NH₂—NHCONH₂)**:
\[\text{R—CHO} + \text{NH}_2\text{NHCONH}_2 \rightarrow \text{R—CH=N—NHCONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}\]
So Z in semicarbazone = **—NHCONH₂** ✓

I = —OH, II = —NHCONH₂
name: Questions Item 10

text:
For which of the following compounds is Kjeldahl's method NOT used for estimation of nitrogen?

I. Aniline  II. Azobenzene  III. Nitrobenzene  IV. Pyridine
explanation:
Kjeldahl's method converts organic N to NH₃ and measures it. It **cannot be used** when:
- N is present in a **double bond** (N=N or N=O)
- N is in a **heterocyclic aromatic ring**

Checking:
- **I (Aniline, C₆H₅NH₂)**: N is in —NH₂ (single bonds only) → **CAN be estimated** ✓ (NOT in the excluded list)
- **II (Azobenzene, C₆H₅—N=N—C₆H₅)**: has —N=N— double bond → **CANNOT be estimated** ✗
- **III (Nitrobenzene, C₆H₅NO₂)**: N has N=O bond → **CANNOT be estimated** ✗
- **IV (Pyridine)**: N is in heterocyclic aromatic ring → **CANNOT be estimated** ✗

Kjeldahl not applicable: **II, III, IV**
name: Questions Item 11

text: Identify the reagents (X, Y, Z) used in the conversion of 3-methylaniline to 3-nitrotoluene.
options: X = NaNO₂, HCl; Y = HBF₄; Z = NaNO₂, Cu, Δ
X = NaNO₃, HCl/273 K; Y = HF; Z = NaNO₃, Cu, Δ
X = NaNO₂, HCl; Y = C₂H₅OH; Z = NaNO₃, Δ
X = NaNO₃, HCl; Y = NaOH; Z = C₆H₅NO₂
explanation:
The conversion of 3-methylaniline (3-aminotoluene) to 3-nitrotoluene proceeds via the diazonium salt:

**Step 1 (X):** Diazotisation at low temperature:
\[\text{3-CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{NH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NaNO}_2, \text{HCl}, 273\text{ K}} \text{3-CH}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^-\]

**Step 2 (Y):** Convert Cl⁻ to BF₄⁻ for the Balz-Schiemann reaction:
\[\text{diazonium Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{HBF}_4} \text{diazonium BF}_4^-\text{ (fluoroborate)}\]

**Step 3 (Z):** Replace diazonium with NO₂ (using NaNO₂ and Cu catalyst):
\[\text{diazonium} \xrightarrow{\text{NaNO}_2, \text{Cu}, \Delta} \text{3-nitrotoluene}\]

X = **NaNO₂, HCl**, Y = **HBF₄**, Z = **NaNO₂, Cu, Δ**
name: Questions Item 12

text:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

\(\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{X} \text{ArH}\)

\(\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{Y} \text{ArH}\)
explanation:
Both reactions convert an aryl diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺Cl⁻) to the **parent arene** (ArH) by **replacing the diazonium group with hydrogen**:

**X = Ethanol (\(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}\)):** The diazonium salt is reduced to ArH by ethanol:
\[\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{ArH} + \text{N}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{HCl}\]

**Y = Hypophosphorous acid (\(\text{H}_3\text{PO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}\)):** Also reduces diazonium to ArH:
\[\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{H}_3\text{PO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{ArH} + \text{N}_2 + \text{HCl} + \text{H}_3\text{PO}_3\]

X = **\(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}\)**, Y = **\(\text{H}_3\text{PO}_2, \text{H}_2\text{O}\)**
name: Questions Item 13

text: The reaction \(\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{Cu} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ArCl} + \text{N}_2 + \text{CuCl}\) is known as
explanation:
The reaction where an **aryl diazonium salt** reacts with **copper metal (Cu)** in the presence of **HCl** to give **chlorobenzene** is the **Gattermann reaction**:

\[\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{Cu} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ArCl} + \text{N}_2 + \text{CuCl}\]

**Sandmeyer reaction** uses **cuprous salts** (\(\text{CuCl}\), \(\text{CuBr}\), \(\text{Cu(CN)}\)) instead of copper metal directly.

- Swarts: fluorination using \(\text{SbF}_3\)
- Stephen: nitrile → aldehyde using \(\text{SnCl}_2/\text{HCl}\)
- Sandmeyer: \(\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{CuCl} \rightarrow \text{ArCl}\) (cuprous salt)
name: Questions Item 14

text:
What are A, B and C in the following reactions?

\(\text{Phthalic acid} + \text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} A \xrightarrow{\text{High temp.}} B \xrightarrow{} C\)
options: A = diammonium salt of phthalic acid; B = phthalamide; C = phthalonitrile
A = phthalamide; B = phthalimide; C = phthalonitrile
A = phthalimide; B = phthalamide; C = phthalonitrile
A = ammonium phthalate; B = phthalamide; C = phthalimide
explanation:
The conversion of phthalic acid to phthalimide:

**Step 1:** Phthalic acid + NH₃ → **Diammonium phthalate (A)**:
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{COOH})_2 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{COONH}_4^+)_2\]

**Step 2:** Heating → **Phthalamide (B)**:
\[\rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{CONH}_2)_2\text{ (dehydration to form amide bonds)}\]

**Step 3:** High temperature → **Phthalimide (C)**:
\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{CONH}_2)_2 \xrightarrow{\text{High T, -NH}_3} \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{CO})_2\text{NH}\text{ (cyclic imide)}\]

A = ammonium phthalate, B = phthalamide, C = **phthalimide**
name: Questions Item 15

text:
What are A and B in the following reaction sequence?

\(\text{Propionitrile} + A \xrightarrow{} B \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} \text{Propiophenone}\)
explanation:
Propiophenone is \(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CO—CH}_2\text{CH}_3\) (phenyl ethyl ketone). Working backwards:

To make a ketone from a nitrile, **phenylmagnesium bromide (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{MgBr}\))** is added to propionitrile:

\[\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C≡N} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{MgBr} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C(=NMgBr)C}_6\text{H}_5}_{B}\]

Hydrolysis of the imine salt:
\[B \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CO—C}_6\text{H}_5}_{\text{propiophenone}}\]

A = **\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{MgBr}\)** (phenyl Grignard), B = the imine-MgBr intermediate
name: Questions Item 16

text:
What are A, B and C in the following reaction?

\(\text{Arene diazonium fluoroborate} \xrightarrow{\text{NaNO}_2/\text{Cu}, \Delta} A + B + C\)
explanation:
When arene diazonium fluoroborate reacts with NaNO₂ and Cu catalyst at elevated temperature:

\[\text{ArN}_2^+\text{BF}_4^- + \text{NaNO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}, \Delta} \underbrace{\text{ArNO}_2}_{A} + \underbrace{\text{N}_2}_{B} + \underbrace{\text{NaBF}_4}_{C}\]

The diazonium group is replaced by the nitro group (—NO₂), nitrogen gas is released, and NaBF₄ is the byproduct from the fluoroborate anion reacting with sodium.

A = **\(\text{ArNO}_2\)** (nitroarene), B = **\(\text{N}_2\)**, C = **\(\text{NaBF}_4\)**
name: Questions Item 17

text: The amine which does NOT react with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide is
explanation:
The reaction of an amine with CHCl₃ and ethanolic KOH to give an **isocyanide** is the **carbylamine reaction** (also called isocyanide test). This reaction is characteristic of **primary amines only**:

\[\text{R—NH}_2 + \text{CHCl}_3 + 3\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{R—NC} + 3\text{KCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

**Secondary amines (R₂NH) do NOT react** because the reaction requires two N—H bonds.

Option (a) \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH(CH}_3)\text{NHCH}_3\) is a **secondary amine** — it has only one N—H bond → **does NOT give carbylamine reaction**.

Options (b), (c), (d) are all primary amines → they do react.
name: Questions Item 18

text:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

\(X \xrightarrow{\text{(i) LiAlH}_4, \text{(ii) H}_2\text{O}} \text{RCH}_2\text{NH}_2 \xleftarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni}} Y\)
explanation:
The target is primary amine **RCH₂NH₂**:

**From X via LiAlH₄ reduction:**
LiAlH₄ reduces both amides and nitriles to primary amines. If the product has RCH₂NH₂ (same R), then X = **\(\text{RCONH}_2\)** (amide) — LiAlH₄ reduces C=O of amide:
\[\text{RCONH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{LiAlH}_4, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{RCH}_2\text{NH}_2\]

**From Y via H₂/Ni catalytic hydrogenation:**
H₂/Ni reduces nitrile (—CN → —CH₂NH₂). Y = **RCN** (nitrile):
\[\text{R—C≡N} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni}} \text{R—CH}_2\text{NH}_2\]

X = **\(\text{RCONH}_2\)**, Y = **RCN**
name: Questions Item 19

text: The order of basic strength of methyl-substituted amines and NH₃ in aqueous solution is
explanation:
In **aqueous solution**, the basic strength of amines is determined by three competing factors:

1. **+I effect of alkyl groups** (more alkyl = stronger base): 3° > 2° > 1° > NH₃
2. **Solvation of ammonium cation** (more solvation = more stable cation = stronger base): 1° > 2° > 3°
3. **Steric hindrance** (more bulky = less accessible N): 3° < 2° < 1°

The combined effect in aqueous solution gives:

\[(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{NH} > \text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2 > (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{N} > \text{NH}_3\]

Dimethylamine (2°) is the strongest because it has a good balance of +I effect and solvation. Trimethylamine (3°) is weaker than expected due to poor solvation and steric effects.
name: Questions Item 20
